title: H1 flows remain positive
gist: Global gold ETFs held on to positive H1 2026 flows (+US$8bn) despite a sharp June reversal, as North American redemptions driven by hawkish Fed signals and US-Iran tensions were offset by record Asian inflows; meanwhile gold market liquidity hit an all-time high and COMEX positioning shows large traders holding firm while retail chases price swings.

insights.claim: Global physically-backed gold ETFs recorded US$8.9bn of outflows in June, cutting AUM 13% to US$526bn and holdings by 74t to 4,047t, yet H1 2026 flows stayed positive at US$8bn.
insights.speaker: @goldcouncil
insights.so_what: The June reversal is a large single-month redemption wave, but it wasn't big enough to flip the H1 trend negative — watch whether July continues the June direction or reverts to the H1 inflow trend.
insights.quote: Global gold ETF flows remained positive at US$8bn in H1.

insights.claim: North America was the only region with net outflows in H1 2026, losing US$7.7bn including US$5.5bn in June alone, marking the region's weakest first half since 2013.
insights.speaker: @goldcouncil
insights.so_what: A 13-year low in North American demand signals US investors are the marginal seller of gold right now, concentrating regional risk to further Fed/rate news.
insights.quote: North American funds lost US$5.5bn in June, bringing the region's H1 outflows to US$7.7bn and resulting in the weakest first half since 2013.

insights.claim: Asia delivered its strongest H1 on record for gold ETF inflows (US$12bn) even after suffering its worst-ever monthly outflow (US$2.3bn) in June, making it the single largest driver of global inflows in H1.
insights.speaker: @goldcouncil
insights.so_what: Asian demand (led by China and India) is now the dominant swing factor for global gold ETF flows, offsetting Western selling — a region to monitor more closely than North America for the next directional signal.
insights.quote: Asia witnessed outflows of US$2.3bn in June, the worst month on record. Despite so, the region experienced their strongest H1 ever, leading global inflows with US$12bn addition.

insights.claim: Within Asia, Chinese fund outflows in June were driven by improving local equity markets pulling risk appetite away from gold, while India bucked the regional trend and saw inflows as investors treated the price dip as a buying opportunity.
insights.speaker: @goldcouncil
insights.so_what: China and India are moving in opposite directions on the same price dip — a rotation-out-of-gold-into-equities story in China versus dip-buying conviction in India, worth tracking as a relative-value signal within Asian demand.
insights.quote: India buckled the trend, attracting inflows in the month as local investors remained optimistic about the gold price and view the dip as entry opportunities.

insights.claim: European gold ETFs saw June outflows of US$818mn, trimming H1 inflows to US$3.2bn, with FX-hedged products (mainly in Switzerland) losing assets as local currency depreciated against the dollar.
insights.speaker: @goldcouncil
insights.so_what: Currency-hedged gold products are exposed to a second channel of loss beyond gold price — a strengthening dollar erodes hedged European (especially Swiss) fund performance independent of bullion moves.

themes.theme: Central bank hawkishness as the outflow trigger

insights.claim: The article attributes North America's record H1 outflows to a chain of causation: a new, hawkish-perceived Fed Chair (Warsh) plus the US-Iran conflict raised inflation fears, which lifted real yields and the dollar, which in turn raised the opportunity cost of holding gold.
insights.speaker: @goldcouncil
insights.so_what: This is a textbook real-yield/opportunity-cost transmission mechanism — any further hawkish surprise from the new Fed Chair or escalation in the US-Iran conflict is a direct headwind for gold ETF demand.
insights.quote: As new Fed Chair Warsh sent hawkish – as the market interpreted – signals and the US-Iran conflict pushed inflation fears up, expectations intensified of higher interest rates ahead.

insights.claim: The European Central Bank hiked rates 25bps in June — its first hike since September 2023 — citing inflation concerns tied to the US-Iran conflict, a move the article says may have deterred some investors from gold.
insights.speaker: @goldcouncil
insights.so_what: A resumption of ECB tightening after a nearly three-year pause is itself a regime-change signal; if sustained it adds a second major central bank (alongside the Fed) working against gold's opportunity-cost case.
insights.quote: the European Central Bank hiked rates by 25bps, the first time since September 2023 citing inflation concerns amid the ongoing US-Iran conflict

insights.claim: The Bank of Japan also hiked rates in June, which the article says pushed up Japanese investors' opportunity cost of holding gold and drove outflows from Japanese funds.
insights.speaker: @goldcouncil
insights.so_what: A synchronized global tightening pulse in June (Fed rhetoric, ECB, BOJ) is the common thread across nearly every region's outflows — a genuinely global, not regional, headwind for gold ETF demand.

insights.claim: The World Gold Council's 2026 Mid-Year Gold Outlook's macro consensus scenario expects relatively stable gold performance in H2, but flags that other scenarios could produce a breakout, while unresolved geopolitical, growth and financial-market uncertainty should keep supporting demand for gold as a safe-haven allocation.
insights.speaker: @goldcouncil
insights.so_what: The base case is a range-bound H2 for gold, but the Council explicitly hedges toward upside tail risk from geopolitical/macro shocks — a call to hold optionality rather than assume flat performance.
insights.quote: The macro consensus scenario in our 2026 Mid-Year Gold Outlook suggests relatively stable gold performances in H2, with potential catalysts possibly brewing a breakout in other scenarios.

insights.claim: Global gold market trading volumes fell 13% m/m in June to US$373bn/day, but the H1 2026 daily average liquidity of US$488bn was the strongest semi-annual average in the World Gold Council's data series, with every major trading segment posting record semi-annual activity.
insights.speaker: @goldcouncil
insights.so_what: Record liquidity even amid falling prices and ETF outflows suggests deepening institutional participation and market structure maturity, not retreat — a supportive backdrop for large trades without excessive price impact.
insights.quote: Global gold market liquidity surged to record levels in H1 at US$488bn per day; the strongest semi-annual average in our data series.

insights.claim: Gold ETF trading volume was the one segment that rose in June even as overall market volumes fell, up 23% m/m to US$6.9bn/day, and averaged US$12bn/day in H1 — up 73% versus the 2025 average, driven mainly by US funds.
insights.speaker: @goldcouncil
insights.so_what: Elevated ETF trading turnover alongside outflows implies more active repositioning/churn (not just passive redemptions) by US investors — a sign of tactical rather than strategic exit behavior.
insights.quote: The notable exception was the gold ETF market, where trading activity increased 23% m/m to US$6.9bn/day, reflecting sustained investor interest.

insights.claim: LBMA trading volumes averaged US$187bn/day in June — 14% below May but still 16% above the 2025 average — and OTC volumes overall stayed well above 2025 levels despite the monthly pullback.
insights.speaker: @goldcouncil
insights.so_what: The June slowdown is a pullback from an elevated base, not a reversion to normal — underlying institutional gold trading activity remains structurally higher than a year ago.

insights.claim: Despite a weaker gold price, COMEX net longs rebounded 16% m/m in June to 538t, the highest month-end level since January, with managed money net longs rising since early June even as the gold price weakened.
insights.speaker: @goldcouncil
insights.so_what: Futures positioning strengthening while price falls and ETFs see redemptions is a divergence signal — leveraged/managed-money traders are accumulating into weakness, which can precede a price floor or squeeze if price stabilizes.
insights.quote: Despite a weaker gold price, total COMEX net longs rebounded by 16% m/m to 538t, the highest month-end level since January.

insights.claim: CFTC positioning data shows a split within COMEX: non-reportable (retail-proxy) net longs fell in June while 'other reportables' (large traders outside the managed-money category) rose 16% m/m, and across H1 retail positioning tracked short-term price moves while larger traders' positions stayed broadly stable since mid-March.
insights.speaker: @goldcouncil
insights.so_what: Retail is trading momentum/price while larger, presumably better-informed traders are holding steady conviction positions — a useful lens for reading future CFTC reports: retail flow noise vs. large-trader signal.
insights.quote: retail positioning largely tracked short-term price movements while larger traders' positions have, in general, stayed stable since mid-March

insights.claim: Managed money net longs were broadly stable across H1 2026, declining by just 43t year-to-date, indicating limited net directional change from this cohort despite the price volatility and ETF flow swings during the half.
insights.speaker: @goldcouncil
insights.so_what: A near-flat H1 for managed money longs against a backdrop of ETF outflows and a falling price suggests futures speculators are not capitulating — positioning risk for a further leg down looks limited from this cohort.
